title: Health psychology: promoting health
summary: Health promotion asks a deceptively simple question: how do you get people to change behaviour that is harming them? This lesson builds the two models the syllabus expects you to know (the health belief model and the transtheoretical/stages-of-change model), the main promotion strategies (fear appeals, education, environmental change) and how effective they actually are, the barriers that defeat well-designed campaigns, and the role of culture. It is built around named supporting studies used — not just described — and a full Paper 2 essay marked against the real IB markband.
learningObjectives: Explain two models of health-behaviour change: the health belief model and the transtheoretical (stages-of-change) model.
Describe the main health-promotion strategies — fear appeals, education, and environmental change — and evaluate how effective each is, using research.
Discuss the barriers to behaviour change and the role of culture in the success of health promotion.
Write and self-mark a Paper 2 essay that USES research rather than just describing it, targeting the top markbands.
levelNote: Common to SL and HL at this level.

simpleExplanation.title: Why telling people the truth rarely changes them
simpleExplanation.summary: Almost every smoker knows smoking is dangerous, yet knowledge alone changes very little. Health promotion is the science of what actually moves behaviour: shaping beliefs, matching the message to how ready a person is to change, and removing the practical obstacles in their way.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think about getting a friend who never exercises to start running. Just texting 'exercise is good for you' does nothing — they already know. What works is making them feel a run is do-able (lend them shoes, start with ten minutes), making the threat feel personal ('your resting heart rate is high'), and clearing the obstacles (run together so it isn't lonely). Health promotion uses exactly these levers at the scale of whole populations.
steps: Name a model of behaviour change (health belief model OR stages of change) and explain what it predicts.
Name a promotion strategy (fear appeal, education, environmental change) and link it to that model.
Bring in a named study and USE its findings to judge whether the strategy works.
Weigh effectiveness — including barriers and culture — and reach a conclusion.

flashcards.back: The process of enabling people to increase control over, and improve, their health (WHO). It targets not only individual behaviour but the social and physical environments that shape it.

A cognitive model (Rosenstock) predicting that a person acts on a health threat when the perceived benefits of acting outweigh the perceived barriers. Six components: perceived susceptibility, perceived severity, perceived benefits, perceived barriers, cues to action, and self-efficacy.

Susceptibility = how likely I think I am to get the condition ('could happen to me'). Severity = how serious I think it would be. Fear appeals usually try to raise BOTH — but raising them without raising self-efficacy tends to backfire.

Belief in one's own capability to carry out a specific behaviour (Bandura). Added to the HBM because knowing a threat is real is useless if you don't believe you can act on it. The single most consistent predictor of behaviour change.

Prochaska & DiClemente's model: change is a process moving through stages — precontemplation → contemplation → preparation → action → maintenance (with possible relapse). Interventions should be matched (tailored) to the person's current stage.

A precontemplator (not even thinking about change) needs information that raises awareness; someone in the action stage needs relapse-prevention skills. Giving the wrong message for the stage — e.g. quit-tips to someone who doesn't want to quit — wastes the intervention.

Persuasive messages that arouse fear of a threat (e.g. graphic cigarette-pack images). Effective ONLY under conditions: moderate fear PLUS a clear, do-able action that raises self-efficacy. Too much fear with no solution causes denial, defensive avoidance or reactance.

Providing information and skills (leaflets, school programmes, mass-media modelling). Raises knowledge reliably but, on its own, changes behaviour weakly — the knowledge–behaviour gap. Strongest when it also builds self-efficacy (e.g. cooking classes, not just 'eat 5 a day').

Changing the context so the healthy choice is the easy/default choice — smoking bans, sugar taxes, plain packaging, removing sweets from checkouts. Often more effective than persuasion because it does not depend on individual motivation, but can be seen as paternalistic.

Obstacles that defeat even well-designed campaigns: practical/structural (cost, access, time), psychological (addiction, low self-efficacy, optimism bias), social (peer norms) and cultural (message clashes with values). Effectiveness depends on removing barriers, not just raising motivation.

flashcards.front: Role of culture in health promotion
flashcards.back: A message that works in one culture can fail or offend in another. Effective campaigns are culturally tailored — matched to language, values, collectivist vs individualist framing, and who counts as a credible source. Ignoring culture is a leading reason imported campaigns fail.

flashcards.front: Exam trap: describing vs USING research
flashcards.back: Naming a study's aim, procedure and findings is DESCRIBING it. USING it means the findings do work in your argument — e.g. 'Because Janis & Feshbach found high fear was LEAST effective, we can conclude a fear appeal must be paired with self-efficacy.' Only USING research reaches the top of Criterion C.

quickCheck.prompt: Which set correctly lists components of the health belief model?
options: Susceptibility, severity, benefits, barriers, cues to action, self-efficacy
Attitude, subjective norms, perceived behavioural control
Precontemplation, contemplation, preparation, action, maintenance
Reward, punishment, modelling, reinforcement
quickCheck.answer: Susceptibility, severity, benefits, barriers, cues to action, self-efficacy

quickCheck.prompt: Research on fear appeals (e.g. Janis & Feshbach; Witte & Allen) most strongly supports which claim?
options: More fear always produces more behaviour change
Fear appeals never work and should be banned
Fear appeals work best at moderate levels AND when paired with a clear, do-able action (self-efficacy)
Fear appeals only work on adults, never on adolescents
quickCheck.answer: Fear appeals work best at moderate levels AND when paired with a clear, do-able action (self-efficacy)

quickCheck.prompt: A campaign posts graphic quit-smoking tips to a population that is mostly 'precontemplation'. In transtheoretical terms, why is this likely to fail?
options: Precontemplators cannot read health messages
The message is stage-mismatched: precontemplators need awareness-raising, not action tips
The transtheoretical model says fear is the only motivator
Precontemplation is the final stage, so change is impossible
quickCheck.answer: The message is stage-mismatched: precontemplators need awareness-raising, not action tips

quickCheck.prompt: An essay names a study's aim, procedure and findings, then moves on without linking it to the strategy's effectiveness. Under the Paper 2 markband this is:
options: Full marks for Criterion C — a study is present
Research DESCRIBED but not USED, which holds Criterion C in the lower bands
Automatically top band for Criterion D
Irrelevant to the markband
quickCheck.answer: Research DESCRIBED but not USED, which holds Criterion C in the lower bands

quickCheck.prompt: A '5 A Day' leaflet campaign raises knowledge but barely changes eating in a low-income area. The best explanation is:
options: The knowledge–behaviour gap: education alone does not remove structural barriers such as cost and access
People in low-income areas cannot understand leaflets
Fear appeals would definitely have worked instead
Knowledge always changes behaviour, so the study must be wrong
quickCheck.answer: The knowledge–behaviour gap: education alone does not remove structural barriers such as cost and access

faq.q: The question says 'strategies' — do I have to evaluate more than one?
faq.a: Yes. For an 'Evaluate strategies for promoting health' essay you should develop at least two strategies (e.g. fear appeals and education, or education and environmental change). Discussing only one narrows your argument and makes it hard to reach the top of Criterion B (knowledge) and Criterion D (critical thinking), because a genuine evaluation of effectiveness usually compares approaches.

faq.q: Do I need two studies, or is one enough?
faq.a: Use at least two studies and USE them. A common markband cap is that research which is only described — not used to support the argument — cannot pull Criterion C into the top band, and a single lightly-handled study leaves Criterion C low. Two studies, each linked to a specific claim about effectiveness, is the safe target.

faq.q: Are fear appeals like graphic cigarette images actually effective?
faq.a: The evidence is conditional, not a simple yes/no — which is exactly what earns critical-thinking marks. Janis & Feshbach (1953) found HIGH fear was LESS effective than moderate/low fear. Witte & Allen's (2000) meta-analysis explains why: fear only changes behaviour when the message also raises self-efficacy and response-efficacy — a clear, achievable action. High fear with no solution triggers denial or defensive avoidance. So 'fear appeals work' is too crude; 'fear appeals work at moderate levels when paired with self-efficacy' is the examinable claim.

faq.q: How is health promotion different from just treating illness?
faq.a: Treatment reacts to disease that already exists; health promotion is proactive and works at the level of populations and environments, not just individuals. It spans primary prevention (stop the problem starting, e.g. anti-smoking campaigns), secondary prevention (early detection, e.g. screening) and tertiary prevention (limiting damage from existing illness, e.g. diabetes self-management support).

sections.content: Almost every smoker can tell you that smoking causes cancer. Almost everyone knows they should eat more vegetables and move more. If knowledge were enough, health promotion would be easy — and it isn't. This lesson is about the gap between knowing and doing: the psychological models that explain it, the strategies designed to close it, the barriers that keep it open, and the research that tells us which strategies actually work. Everything here is built to be USED in a Paper 2 essay, not just recited.

The syllabus expects you to be able to explain how psychologists model the decision to change behaviour. Two models do most of the work in exam answers. Both are worth knowing well because they explain WHY a given strategy should — or shouldn't — work, which is the difference between describing a campaign and evaluating it.

items: **Health belief model (HBM — Rosenstock).** A cognitive model: a person is more likely to act on a health threat when they perceive high **susceptibility** ('this could happen to me') and high **severity** ('it would be serious'), see clear **benefits** to acting that outweigh the **barriers**, receive a **cue to action** (an external prompt), and have the **self-efficacy** to carry the action out. Its strength is that it names the levers a campaign can pull; its weakness is that it treats behaviour as coldly rational and ignores habit, addiction and emotion.

**Transtheoretical / stages-of-change model (Prochaska & DiClemente).** Change is not a single event but a process through stages: **precontemplation** (not considering change) → **contemplation** (thinking about it) → **preparation** → **action** → **maintenance**, with **relapse** as a normal loop. The practical payoff: interventions should be **stage-matched** — a precontemplator needs awareness-raising, while someone in the action stage needs relapse-prevention skills. Giving the right message at the wrong stage wastes it.

**How the models connect to strategy.** A fear appeal is essentially an attempt to spike perceived susceptibility and severity in the HBM; an education programme tries to raise benefits and self-efficacy; environmental change lowers barriers directly. Naming this link is what turns a description of a campaign into an explanation of it.

sections.content: Strategies for promoting health — and how well they work
There are three broad strategies the syllabus highlights. Notice that they escalate in how much they depend on the individual's own motivation — and that dependence is a clue to their effectiveness.

items: **Fear appeals.** Arouse fear of a threat to motivate change (graphic pack images, shock road-safety adverts). Effective only under conditions: moderate rather than extreme fear, and — crucially — a clear, do-able action that raises self-efficacy. Without that, high fear produces denial, defensive avoidance or reactance (doing the opposite).

**Education.** Provide information and skills — school programmes, leaflets, mass-media modelling. Reliably raises knowledge, but knowledge alone changes behaviour weakly (the knowledge–behaviour gap). Strongest when it also builds self-efficacy and skills, not just facts.

**Environmental change.** Reshape the context so the healthy option becomes the easy or default one: indoor smoking bans, sugar/tobacco taxes, plain packaging, removing sweets from checkouts. Because it does not rely on persuading each individual, it is often the MOST effective strategy — but it raises debates about paternalism and freedom.

**The general finding.** Across the literature, strategies that change the environment or build self-efficacy tend to outperform strategies that rely on information or fear alone. Effectiveness is conditional, which is the point examiners want you to argue — not 'campaigns work' but 'campaigns work WHEN…'.

sections.question: Worked example 1 — Explain the health belief model and use it to evaluate ONE fear-appeal campaign.
sections.solution:
**Step 1 — State the model precisely.** The health belief model (Rosenstock) predicts that a person acts on a health threat when perceived susceptibility and severity are high, perceived benefits outweigh perceived barriers, a cue to action is present, and self-efficacy is sufficient.

**Step 2 — Map the strategy onto the model.** Graphic warning images on cigarette packs are a fear appeal designed to raise **perceived severity** (this is what lung cancer does) and **perceived susceptibility** (this could be you). The pack itself acts as a repeated **cue to action** at the exact moment of temptation.

**Step 3 — USE a study to judge effectiveness.** Janis & Feshbach (1953) manipulated the level of fear in a dental-hygiene talk and found the HIGH-fear group changed their brushing behaviour LEAST, while the low/moderate-fear group changed most. Applying this: a pack image that maximises severity may over-shoot, triggering defensive avoidance, so the fear component alone is not enough.

**Step 4 — Add the missing HBM component.** The HBM says self-efficacy is required. Witte & Allen's (2000) meta-analysis confirms fear appeals only change behaviour when paired with a message that the recommended action is achievable. This is why effective pack designs pair the image with a quitline number — a low-barrier, high-self-efficacy action.

**Conclusion:** The campaign is effective to the extent that it raises severity/susceptibility AND supplies a do-able action; the HBM predicts, and Janis & Feshbach supports, that severity without self-efficacy backfires.

sections.question: Worked example 2 — Discuss barriers to behaviour change and the role of culture, using a healthy-eating strategy.
sections.solution:
**Strategy:** a '5 A Day'-style education campaign promoting five portions of fruit and vegetables daily.

**Barrier analysis.** Education reliably raises knowledge but runs into the knowledge–behaviour gap. Structural barriers — cost, distance to shops selling fresh produce ('food deserts'), lack of storage or time — mean that in low-income communities, knowing the '5 A Day' message does not translate into eating it. In transtheoretical terms, many people are stuck between contemplation and action precisely because barriers block the action stage. So an information-only strategy is predicted to be weak where barriers are high.

**USING research.** A study of low-income UK participants (Dibsdall et al., 2003) found that practical barriers — cost, transport, storage — were the dominant obstacles to healthy eating, largely independent of knowledge or intention. USED as evidence: this shows education alone cannot succeed without environmental change (e.g. subsidies, local access), because the limiting factor is not motivation but opportunity.

**Role of culture.** Effectiveness also depends on cultural fit. Food choices carry cultural meaning, and a generic message framed around individual self-improvement may not resonate in collectivist contexts where family and community norms drive eating. A culturally tailored campaign — matching language, foods, framing and credible messengers — outperforms an imported one. Ignoring culture is a leading reason campaigns that succeed in one country fail in another.

**Conclusion:** The effectiveness of a healthy-eating strategy is bounded by barriers and culture. The strongest programmes combine education (building self-efficacy through skills such as cooking classes) with environmental change (improving access/affordability) and cultural tailoring — not information alone.

sections.content: The verb 'evaluate' means make an appraisal by weighing strengths and limitations and reaching a judgement about EFFECTIVENESS. Do not just list features of a campaign. For every strategy you name, do three things: link it to a model (HBM or stages of change), attach a study whose findings you actually USE, and state a conditional conclusion ('this works WHEN…'). That conditional judgement is what lifts Criterion D.

items: **Describing a study instead of USING it.** Reciting aim/procedure/findings and moving on leaves Criterion C in the lower bands. Every study must earn its place by supporting a specific claim about effectiveness ('Because Janis & Feshbach found…, we can conclude…').
**Claiming 'fear appeals work' (or 'never work').** Both are wrong and cost critical-thinking marks. The examinable claim is conditional: moderate fear PLUS self-efficacy. State the condition.

**Muddling the HBM components.** Susceptibility ('could happen to me') is not severity ('would be serious'), and self-efficacy is not a cue to action. Mislabelling components signals shallow knowledge and caps Criterion B.

**Treating stages of change as fixed or one-way.** Relapse is a normal loop, not a failure of the model, and interventions must be stage-MATCHED. Giving action-stage advice to a precontemplator is a classic error.

**Evaluating the STUDIES but never the STRATEGY.** 'The sample was small, it lacked ecological validity…' about the research is not the same as judging whether the health strategy is effective. The question is about strategies — keep the evaluation aimed at effectiveness.
**Ignoring barriers and culture.** Concluding a campaign 'works' without acknowledging structural barriers or cultural fit produces a one-sided answer that cannot reach the top of Criterion D.

sections.content: Model answer — marked against the real IB Paper 2 markband

sections.question: Evaluate strategies for promoting health. [22]
sections.solution:
**Model essay**

**Thesis.** Strategies for promoting health are effective only under specific conditions rather than universally. This essay evaluates two of the most widely used strategies — fear appeals and education — arguing that each succeeds only when it is grounded in a model of behaviour change (the health belief model and the transtheoretical model) and only when it addresses self-efficacy, barriers and culture. Research shows that the strategies most dependent on individual motivation alone are the least reliable.

**Strategy 1 — Fear appeals, explained through the health belief model.** A fear appeal arouses fear of a threat to motivate change, for example graphic images on cigarette packaging. In terms of the health belief model (Rosenstock), such campaigns target perceived severity ('this is what smoking does to you') and perceived susceptibility ('this could be you'), with the pack serving as a repeated cue to action. The model predicts, however, that severity and susceptibility are not sufficient on their own: benefits must outweigh barriers and the person must have the self-efficacy to act.

This prediction is directly supported by Janis & Feshbach (1953). Their aim was to test whether increasing fear increases persuasion. They gave school students a talk on dental hygiene manipulated into high-, moderate- and low-fear versions (the high-fear version used graphic images of diseased gums), then measured changes in brushing behaviour a week later. The finding was counter-intuitive and important: the LOW-fear group showed the greatest behaviour change and the HIGH-fear group the least. USED as evidence, this tells us that maximising fear in a health campaign can be self-defeating — extreme fear appears to trigger defensive avoidance rather than action, so a fear appeal that pushes severity too hard will underperform.

Why does moderate fear work while high fear fails? Witte & Allen's (2000) meta-analysis of fear-appeal studies provides the mechanism. Their finding was that fear appeals change behaviour only when the message is accompanied by high response-efficacy and self-efficacy — a recommended action that is both effective and achievable. USED here, this converts a vague claim into a precise one: a graphic pack image works when it is paired with a low-barrier action such as a quitline number, and backfires when it presents an overwhelming threat with no route out. This is exactly the self-efficacy component the health belief model requires, now confirmed across many studies.

**Strategy 2 — Education, and its limits.** Education strategies provide information and skills through school programmes, leaflets and mass media. They reliably raise knowledge, but knowledge changes behaviour weakly — the knowledge–behaviour gap. The transtheoretical model (Prochaska & DiClemente) explains part of this: because people move through stages (precontemplation → contemplation → preparation → action → maintenance), a single, un-tailored educational message will be stage-mismatched for much of its audience. Awareness-raising suits precontemplators; relapse-prevention suits people in maintenance; a leaflet aimed at everyone reaches no one well.

Education also collides with barriers. USED as evidence, Dibsdall et al.'s (2003) study of low-income UK participants found that practical barriers — cost, transport to shops with fresh produce, and storage — were the main obstacles to healthy eating, largely independent of knowledge or intention. The implication for an education strategy is decisive: telling people to 'eat 5 a day' cannot work where the limiting factor is opportunity, not information. Education becomes effective only when it also builds self-efficacy (skills such as cooking classes) and is combined with environmental change that removes the barriers — for example subsidies or improved local access. Culture compounds this: a message framed around individual choice may not resonate in collectivist contexts, so culturally tailored campaigns outperform imported ones.

**Developed evaluation of effectiveness.** Comparing the two strategies yields a clear appraisal. Fear appeals can work, but only at moderate intensity and only when paired with self-efficacy — a narrow and easily-missed window, as Janis & Feshbach and Witte & Allen together show. Education raises knowledge dependably but changes behaviour only when it is stage-matched, self-efficacy-building and combined with the removal of structural barriers, as Dibsdall et al. imply. A consistent pattern emerges: strategies that lean on individual motivation alone (fear, information) are the least reliable, while strategies that lower barriers or build capability are the most reliable. This is also why environmental-change measures — smoking bans, taxes, plain packaging — often outperform persuasion, though they raise fair objections about paternalism. Two limitations qualify even this conclusion. First, much of the evidence is correlational (real-world campaigns run alongside tax changes and legislation), so isolating a single strategy's effect is difficult. Second, effectiveness is culture-bound, so findings from one population may not generalise.

**Conclusion.** No strategy for promoting health is effective in the abstract. Fear appeals work at moderate levels with self-efficacy; education works when stage-matched and combined with skill-building and barrier removal; both must be culturally tailored. The soundest judgement is that the most effective health promotion combines a model-driven message with environmental change that makes the healthy choice the easy choice — because behaviour follows opportunity and capability at least as much as it follows knowledge or fear.

---
**How this maps to our IB markband (Paper 2 essay, out of 22)**

- **A — Focus on the question (2/2).** The thesis names 'strategies' (plural) and frames the whole essay around EVALUATING effectiveness, and every paragraph returns to that judgement. The command term 'evaluate' is answered, not sidestepped.
- **B — Knowledge and understanding (6/6).** Two strategies AND two models (health belief model; transtheoretical) are explained accurately, with components used correctly (susceptibility vs severity; self-efficacy; the stages). Terminology is precise throughout.
- **C — Use of research to support the answer (6/6).** Three studies are present and, crucially, USED: Janis & Feshbach and Witte & Allen support the conditional claim about fear, and Dibsdall et al. supports the barriers/education claim. Each is linked to a specific point ('USED as evidence, this tells us…') rather than described and dropped. This clears the two common caps: research is USED, and more than one study is developed.
- **D — Critical thinking (6/6).** The evaluation is DEVELOPED, not listed: it compares strategies, explains the mechanism (why high fear backfires; why knowledge ≠ behaviour), weighs environmental change against paternalism, and flags correlational and cultural limitations. Judgements are conditional and justified.
- **E — Clarity and organisation (2/2).** Clear thesis, one idea per paragraph, signposted structure, and a conclusion that synthesises rather than repeats.

Why this reaches the top bands: the answer never lets a study sit as decoration and never states an unconditional 'it works'. Every study does argumentative work, and every claim about effectiveness is qualified by evidence — the two moves that separate top-band C and D from the middle.

sections.content: This is exactly what the Practice button does: write your own answer to 'Evaluate strategies for promoting health. [22]', submit it, and our marking engine grades it against this same official Paper 2 markband, telling you which band you hit on each criterion and why. Watch the two caps that quietly cost most marks: (1) Criterion C — research must be USED to support a point, not just described, or it stays in the lower bands; and (2) Criterion D — critical thinking must be DEVELOPED into a judgement about effectiveness, not a generic list of study flaws. Fix those two and you move up the bands fastest.
